content.text: When a language model works through a complex problem, the path from question to answer is rarely straight.

give the model a designated private workspace — a hidden channel inside its generation where it can draft hypotheses, revise plans, and perform intermediate calculations before committing to a polished final response. The orchestrating application strips the scratchpad content before delivering the answer to the end user, preserving the accuracy benefits of chain-of-thought reasoning without exposing raw, messy deliberation.

A scratchpad is a partitioned section of the model's output — or, in provider-native implementations, a dedicated API field — where the model performs intermediate reasoning that is intentionally excluded from the final user-facing response. The concept extends chain-of-thought (CoT) by adding an output boundary: everything before the boundary is private working memory; everything after it is the deliverable.
The most common implementation uses XML-style delimiters specified in the system prompt. The model is instructed to write all exploratory reasoning inside
tags and its final answer inside

tags. The orchestrating layer then applies a simple extraction rule — take everything between the answer tags, discard the rest from the user-facing response, and optionally store the full output in an internal log. A newer alternative is provider-level extended thinking, where the API separates scratchpad tokens from completion tokens in the response object itself, requiring no delimiter parsing.

Scratchpads should not be confused with external memory systems or tool call results. They are purely generative: the model produces the scratchpad text itself, token by token, as part of a single inference pass. The scratchpad is not an external database or a separate API call — it is simply a defined region of the model's output that is treated differently by the application layer.

Unfiltered chain-of-thought significantly improves model accuracy on multi-step tasks, but it introduces a serious UX problem: the raw reasoning output is often verbose, self-contradictory, and written in an exploratory style that confuses end users. A model reasoning through a financial calculation might write 'wait, I made an error, let me redo this' — which is helpful for accuracy but alarming if a banking customer sees it as the assistant's response.

Beyond UX, there are regulatory and confidentiality concerns. In healthcare, finance, and legal applications, the reasoning process may contain intermediate conclusions, rule applications, or uncertainty estimates that regulators or legal counsel may not want surfaced to the end user without review. Scratchpads let organizations capture and audit reasoning internally while presenting a clean, reviewed output externally.

In multi-agent pipelines, scratchpads provide a particularly important function: they give each agent a safe space to reason about the messages it has received from other agents before deciding how to respond or what action to take. Without a private reasoning channel, the agent must either expose its deliberation as part of its outbound message (adding noise to inter-agent communication) or suppress reasoning entirely (reducing accuracy). Scratchpads resolve this tension.

The primary advantage is improved answer quality without degraded user experience. Extended deliberation consistently raises accuracy on tasks involving multi-step arithmetic, logical deduction, planning, and code generation — and the scratchpad ensures this comes at no cost to readability of the final answer.

Allowing the model to reason freely before committing reduces premature conclusions. Benchmarks for tasks such as GSM8K (grade-school math) and HotpotQA (multi-hop reasoning) show that extended thinking with private scratchpads matches or exceeds standard visible CoT.
The delivered answer is free from hedges, crossed-out attempts, and exploratory digressions. Users receive what they actually need without filtering noise.

The full scratchpad is retained in server-side logs, enabling engineers to diagnose incorrect answers, identify reasoning failures, and improve prompts — without any of that diagnostic data reaching end users.

Malicious content in user inputs or tool outputs that attempts to hijack the model's reasoning is contained within the scratchpad. The final answer is extracted structurally, so injected instructions that alter scratchpad content but not the answer tags are less likely to surface.
Systems can allocate more tokens to the scratchpad for difficult requests and fewer for simple ones, optimizing cost per call without changing the answer format.
In multi-agent pipelines, agents communicate via their answer section only. Their reasoning stays private, keeping orchestrator context windows clean.

Scratchpads are not cost-free. Every token generated in the scratchpad consumes inference compute and API cost even though it is invisible to the user. For providers that charge equally for all output tokens (including thinking tokens), a scratchpad of 500 tokens adds directly to the per-call cost. Latency increases proportionally — a model that writes 1,000 scratchpad tokens before answering takes noticeably longer to respond than one that answers directly.
Scratchpad content is billed as output tokens even though users never see it. This can double or triple the effective cost per query for complex tasks.
Time-to-first-token for the answer section is delayed by the scratchpad generation time. For streaming UIs, this means users wait longer before seeing any output.
Native reasoning token support (e.g., Anthropic extended thinking) is not universally available. Delimiter-based fallbacks require careful prompt engineering and robust parsing.
The system prompt must clearly specify the scratchpad protocol. Poorly specified instructions lead to models mixing reasoning and answer content, breaking the extraction logic.
Teams may add scratchpads to every request, even simple ones where a direct answer is faster and cheaper. The pattern should be applied selectively.
The scratchpad is generated by the same model and is not a privileged truth channel. The model can still produce flawed or confused reasoning. It is a workspace, not a verification layer.

At inference time, the system prompt instructs the model about the two-phase output structure. The model generates tokens sequentially, producing the scratchpad section first (reasoning, planning, checking) and the answer section second (the final response). From the model's perspective, this is a single autoregressive generation pass — it cannot 'go back' to the scratchpad once it has emitted the answer delimiter. This forward-only constraint means the scratchpad must be thorough enough before the model transitions to the answer.
The extraction logic on the application side is straightforward: scan the full completion for the opening and closing answer tags (e.g.,

) and return the content between them. Everything outside these tags — including the entire scratchpad section — is either discarded or written to an internal log. If the model fails to produce an answer tag (a generation failure mode), the extraction falls back to returning the full completion or raising an error.

Token budget management is a key operational concern. Scratchpad tokens are part of the same context window as the answer tokens. Allowing unlimited scratchpad generation can push the answer into the tail of the context window, where models may truncate or degrade. Best practice is to specify a soft token budget in the system prompt ('use at most 800 tokens for your thinking') and monitor scratchpad lengths in production.
In provider-native implementations such as Anthropic's extended thinking, the API handles the separation automatically. The response object contains a
field with the reasoning content and a
field with the answer. No delimiter parsing is needed, and the provider may optimize token allocation between the two sections internally.

The diagram below shows the two-channel output model. The user prompt flows into a single LLM inference call. The model generates two partitioned regions: the private scratchpad (reasoning workspace) and the public final answer. The orchestrator applies extraction logic, delivers the answer to the user, and routes the full output (including scratchpad) to an internal audit log.

attrs.alt: Scratchpad architecture: User Prompt flows into LLM Inference which produces a private Scratchpad section and a public Final Answer section. Orchestrator extracts the answer for the user and logs the full output internally.
attrs.title: The scratchpad and answer are generated in a single inference pass; the orchestrator partitions them at read time.

content.text: A critical architectural property is that the scratchpad is

. The model constructs it fresh each time, which means it can adapt the style, depth, and focus of its reasoning to the specific prompt. There is no persistent scratchpad state between calls unless the application explicitly includes prior scratchpad content in a subsequent prompt (an advanced technique for multi-turn reasoning continuity).

Scratchpads are most valuable when the task requires multi-step reasoning that would be distracting or confusing if shown directly, and when accuracy on that reasoning materially affects the quality of the final answer.
Math problems, legal analysis, medical triage support, debugging code, and multi-constraint planning all benefit from extended deliberation that users should not see.
When the reasoning process must be audited by engineers but not exposed to end users or third parties.
Where raw CoT output would degrade trust or create confusion ('the AI seems unsure of itself').
Each agent uses a private scratchpad to evaluate peer messages, check for inconsistencies, and plan its next action before sending a clean response upstream.
The agent uses the scratchpad to interpret tool results (which may be raw JSON or error messages) and decide whether to call another tool, retry, or synthesize an answer.
If the task is a factual lookup or a single-operation transformation, scratchpad tokens add cost and latency with minimal accuracy gain.
If the application requires sub-second responses and handles thousands of requests per minute, the token overhead of scratchpads may be prohibitive.
When the reasoning itself is the product.
Tutoring, Socratic learning, and explainability applications specifically need the reasoning visible. A scratchpad would defeat the purpose.
Step 1 — define the output protocol in the system prompt
The system prompt is the single source of truth for the scratchpad convention. Specify the delimiter tags, the purpose of each section, and any token budget guidance. Be explicit and consistent.

You are a reasoning assistant. Before answering, think through the problem step by step.

Output format (strict):
<thinking>
[Your private working notes. Explore the problem, list sub-steps, check assumptions, draft and revise.
Do not hold back — this section is for reasoning, not for presentation.
Use at most 600 tokens in this section.]
</thinking>
<answer>
[Your final, polished response. This is the only part the user will see.
Be concise, accurate, and complete.]
</answer>

Always produce both sections. Never skip the <thinking> block, even for simple questions.
Step 2 — extract the answer in the application layer

Parse the model's completion to extract content between the answer tags. The extraction must handle edge cases: missing tags (generation failure or prompt not followed), nested tags (unlikely but possible), and leading/trailing whitespace.

import re
from typing import Optional

def extract_answer(completion: str) -> tuple[str, Optional[str]]:
    """
    Returns (answer_text, scratchpad_text).
    answer_text is the public response; scratchpad_text is for internal logging.
    If no <answer> tags are found, returns the full completion as the answer.
    """
    thinking_match = re.search(r'<thinking>(.*?)</thinking>', completion, re.DOTALL)
    answer_match   = re.search(r'<answer>(.*?)</answer>', completion, re.DOTALL)

    scratchpad = thinking_match.group(1).strip() if thinking_match else None
    answer     = answer_match.group(1).strip()   if answer_match   else completion.strip()

    return answer, scratchpad


# Usage example
completion = """
<thinking>
The user asked for the square root of 144.
144 = 12 * 12, so sqrt(144) = 12.
Let me double-check: 12^2 = 144. Correct.
</thinking>
<answer>
The square root of 144 is **12**.
</answer>
"""

answer, scratch = extract_answer(completion)
print(f"Answer delivered to user: {answer}")
print(f"Scratchpad logged internally: {scratch[:60]}...")

Store the scratchpad alongside the answer and request metadata in a logging system. This enables post-hoc analysis of reasoning failures, prompt regression testing, and latency profiling of scratchpad length vs. answer quality.

import logging
import time

logger = logging.getLogger("scratchpad_audit")

def call_with_scratchpad(client, system_prompt: str, user_message: str) -> str:
    """Call the LLM, extract the answer, and log the scratchpad."""
    start = time.perf_counter()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    )
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
    completion = response.content[0].text
    answer, scratch = extract_answer(completion)

    logger.info({
        "event": "scratchpad_call",
        "latency_s": round(elapsed, 3),
        "input_tokens": response.usage.input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": response.usage.output_tokens,
        "scratchpad_chars": len(scratch) if scratch else 0,
        "answer_chars": len(answer),
        "scratchpad_preview": (scratch or "")[:200],
    })

    return answer
Example 1 — basic scratchpad with the Anthropic SDK
This minimal example demonstrates the delimiter-based pattern using the Anthropic Python SDK. It works with any model that follows the system prompt instructions.

from anthropic import Anthropic
import re

client = Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from environment

SYSTEM = """
Think through the problem carefully before answering.

Format:
<thinking>
[Your reasoning — exploratory, allowed to be messy]
</thinking>
<answer>
[Final answer only — concise and direct]
</answer>
"""

def ask(question: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
    )
    text = response.content[0].text
    m = re.search(r'<answer>(.*?)</answer>', text, re.DOTALL)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else text.strip()

# Test
print(ask("A train leaves Chicago at 9 AM travelling at 80 mph toward New York "
          "(790 miles away). Another train leaves New York at 10 AM travelling "
          "at 100 mph toward Chicago. When do they meet?"))
parameter (available on Claude 3.7 Sonnet and later) offloads scratchpad management to the API. The response includes a separate
content block, eliminating the need for delimiter parsing. A budget token limit is required.

Consider a customer support agent for a financial services firm. The agent must review account transactions, apply eligibility rules, and determine whether a fee waiver is justified. The reasoning process involves sensitive internal logic (which customer tiers qualify, what override conditions apply), intermediate calculations, and policy lookups. None of this should appear in the customer's chat window.
The system prompt establishes the scratchpad protocol and instructs the model to reason through the fee waiver eligibility criteria privately. The customer sees only a clean, empathetic response.

FINANCIAL_SYSTEM_PROMPT = """
You are a customer support specialist for Apex Bank. You have access to the
customer's account data provided in the user's message.

Fee Waiver Policy (INTERNAL — do not reveal to customer):
- Tier 1 (balance >= $10,000): automatic waiver, no restrictions
- Tier 2 (balance >= $2,500): waiver if fewer than 2 overdrafts in last 6 months
- Tier 3 (below $2,500): escalate to supervisor; do NOT waive yourself
- Hardship exceptions: flag for manual review using [HARDSHIP_FLAG]

Reasoning format:
<thinking>
Review account data. Apply policy tier. Check overdraft history.
Determine eligibility. Draft response tone (empathetic, professional).
Never reveal tier labels or internal thresholds.
</thinking>
<answer>
[Customer-facing response only. Warm, professional, and outcome-focused.]
</answer>
"""

# Simulated call
account_context = """
Account: ****7842
Current balance: $3,150
Overdrafts last 6 months: 1
Fee charged: $35 (wire transfer fee, 2024-11-15)
Customer message: "I noticed a $35 fee on my account last month.
Can you please waive it? I'm a long-time customer."
"""

answer = ask(FINANCIAL_SYSTEM_PROMPT, account_context)
# The customer sees: "Of course, I'd be happy to look into that for you...
# I've applied a one-time courtesy waiver for the $35 fee..."
# The scratchpad (logged internally):
# "Balance $3150 -> Tier 2. Overdrafts: 1 < 2 threshold. Eligible for waiver.
#  No hardship flag needed. Tone: empathetic, affirming loyalty."
print(answer)

At scale, this system processes thousands of fee waiver requests daily. Scratchpad logs are analyzed weekly to detect policy misapplication, identify prompt drift, and tune the eligibility reasoning. Engineers use the scratchpad audit trail to catch edge cases (e.g., the model sometimes miscounts overdrafts in months with multiple transactions) without surfacing diagnostic noise to customers.
Mistake 1 — echoing the scratchpad in the answer

The model sometimes re-states reasoning steps in the answer section. This happens when the system prompt does not clearly separate the purposes of each section. The fix is to explicitly tell the model what the answer must not contain.

# Bad system prompt (incomplete boundary)
Think first, then answer.

# Better system prompt (explicit boundary)
<thinking> block: Reasoning only. Never addressed to the user.
<answer> block: Final response only. No reasoning steps. Speak directly to the user.
Do not start the answer with phrases like 'After thinking through this...'

Teams discard the scratchpad to save storage, then discover they cannot diagnose production errors because the reasoning is gone. The scratchpad is the primary debugging artifact. Log it always; storage cost for text is negligible.

Without a budget, the model may generate thousands of scratchpad tokens on complex tasks, consuming the context window and increasing cost unpredictably. Always set a budget in the system prompt and monitor p95 scratchpad length in production.
Mistake 4 — using the scratchpad as a tool call substitute

Some implementations ask the model to simulate tool calls inside the scratchpad (e.g., 'In my scratchpad, I will look up the customer's account'). This does not actually call any tool — it is just the model generating text. Actual tool calls must go through the API's function-calling mechanism; the scratchpad is for reasoning about real tool results, not fabricating them.
Using different tag names across prompts (
in others) forces the extraction layer to handle multiple formats. Standardize on one convention and enforce it as a project-wide constant.
) shared across all prompt templates. Never hard-code tag strings in multiple places.
Set and monitor a scratchpad token budget.
Use a soft budget in the prompt ('use at most N tokens') and a hard limit via
. Alert when p95 scratchpad length exceeds 80% of the budget.
Include request ID, model version, prompt version, latency, scratchpad length, and answer length in every log entry. This enables regressions and prompt A/B testing.
Validate extraction in your test suite.
Unit-test the extraction function with malformed completions: missing tags, swapped tag order, nested tags, and empty sections. The extraction must be robust before going to production.

When iterating on prompts, always inspect the scratchpad output. The quality of the reasoning is the leading indicator of answer quality — if the scratchpad is confused, the answer will be wrong even if it looks confident.
Native extended thinking (Anthropic) is more reliable than delimiter-based approaches because the provider enforces the separation at the generation level, not in the output string.
Do not include scratchpad content in multi-turn conversation history.

When building conversation history for subsequent turns, include only the answer section. Sending scratchpad tokens as prior assistant messages wastes context and can confuse the model about what is public vs. private.

The dominant performance cost of scratchpads is output token consumption. If the average scratchpad is 400 tokens and the average answer is 200 tokens, scratchpad tokens account for 67% of all output tokens — and therefore 67% of output token billing. At scale, this is material. Teams should profile the scratchpad-to-answer token ratio across their request distribution and tune the prompt budget to minimize unnecessary scratchpad expansion.

Latency is proportional to total output tokens. A scratchpad of 500 tokens adds roughly the same latency as an answer of 500 tokens. For streaming UIs, this means users see nothing until the model finishes the scratchpad and begins the answer section. One mitigation is to stream the answer section directly as the model generates it by detecting the opening answer tag in the stream and beginning to forward tokens to the client from that point.

async def stream_answer_only(client, system_prompt: str, question: str):
    """Stream only the <answer> section to the client, discarding <thinking>."""
    in_answer = False
    buffer = ""

    async with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
    ) as stream:
        async for text in stream.text_stream:
            buffer += text
            if not in_answer:
                if "<answer>" in buffer:
                    in_answer = True
                    # Emit everything after the opening tag
                    _, after = buffer.split("<answer>", 1)
                    yield after
                    buffer = ""
            else:
                if "</answer>" in buffer:
                    # Emit up to the closing tag
                    before, _ = buffer.split("</answer>", 1)
                    yield before
                    return
                else:
                    yield buffer
                    buffer = ""

For high-throughput applications, caching the system prompt using provider-level prompt caching (Anthropic cache_control or OpenAI prompt caching) reduces the marginal cost of each request. The system prompt — which is identical across all requests — is cached after the first call, so only the user message and scratchpad/answer tokens are billed at full rate.
Several frameworks and provider features support scratchpad patterns out of the box or with minimal configuration.
Available on Claude 3.7 Sonnet (and later models). The

integer. The model decides internally how to allocate tokens between thinking and response. The thinking content is returned in a separate content block and is not billed as visible output in the same way as standard text.
as a model kwarg. For delimiter-based scratchpads, LangChain's output parsers can be configured to extract structured content from completions.

In LangGraph agent graphs, each node function can invoke the LLM with a scratchpad protocol and store the extracted scratchpad in the graph state for debugging. The answer is passed downstream while the scratchpad is attached to the state but excluded from inter-node communication.

OpenAI's o1 and o3 model families use a similar approach: reasoning tokens are generated before the visible completion but are not returned in the API response (they are used internally). Unlike Anthropic's extended thinking, the reasoning content is not accessible to developers. The pattern is conceptually identical but the scratchpad is fully opaque.
Several techniques address the accuracy-vs-readability tension. The table below compares them across key dimensions.

The scratchpad is generated by the same model and is subject to the same adversarial manipulation risks as the full completion. A prompt injection attack embedded in a tool result or user message can cause the model to write malicious instructions in its scratchpad. If those instructions alter the answer section, the extraction logic will faithfully deliver the manipulated answer. The scratchpad does not provide isolation from prompt injection — it only provides output partitioning.

Scratchpad content must be treated as untrusted input if it is ever re-used (e.g., in a subsequent prompt turn). Do not allow the scratchpad from one call to drive downstream actions without sanitization. In particular, do not pass raw scratchpad content as instructions to another agent.

Reliability concerns center on compliance: how reliably does the model actually follow the output format? Models occasionally omit the thinking section, merge reasoning into the answer, or produce malformed tag structures. Extraction code must handle all failure modes gracefully. A useful production heuristic is to track the format compliance rate per model version and alert when it drops below threshold (e.g., 99.5% of responses have valid answer tags).
If the answer tag is absent, fall back to the full completion rather than returning an empty string. Log the format failure for investigation.
Do not expose scratchpad to users via error messages.
If extraction fails and the application falls back to the full completion, ensure the fallback handler does not accidentally surface thinking content in an error payload.
Scratchpad logs may contain sensitive intermediate reasoning (e.g., internal policy thresholds, customer data in context). Apply the same access controls as to the raw API response logs.
Pin extraction to the model version.
If a model update changes the default format of reasoning output, the extraction regex can silently break. Pin model versions in production and run extraction regression tests before upgrading.
A scratchpad partitions model output into a private reasoning channel and a public answer channel, enabling extended deliberation without exposing it to end users.

The two implementation approaches are delimiter-based (XML tags in the system prompt, parsed client-side) and provider-native (Anthropic extended thinking, OpenAI reasoning tokens), each with different auditability and cost profiles.
Scratchpad tokens are billed at the same rate as answer tokens; always set a token budget in the prompt and monitor scratchpad length in production to control cost.
Log the full scratchpad output alongside every request — it is the primary debugging artifact for reasoning failures and prompt regressions.
The scratchpad is not a security boundary: prompt injection attacks in tool outputs or user messages can still influence both the scratchpad and the final answer.
Extraction code must be robust to missing, malformed, or swapped tags; test all failure modes explicitly before production deployment.

In multi-agent systems, each agent's scratchpad keeps inter-agent communication clean by confining deliberation to the private channel and forwarding only the structured answer to peers and orchestrators.
Do not include scratchpad content in subsequent conversation turns as prior assistant messages — only the answer section should form the conversation history passed to the next call.
